Royal Navy Type 42s went without an antiship missile fit because the additional capability was judged expendable when costs were reviewed. Argentina selected a different configuration for ships built from the same basic design. The comparison shows how export variants can expose national procurement priorities: a shared hull does not imply identical weapons when customers fund different missions.
segments.question: Why did Royal Navy Type 42 destroyers lack antiship missiles when Argentine Type 42s carried them?
segments.answerShort: Britain saved the money; Argentina paid for a different weapons fit on the same basic destroyer design.

evidence.note: The answer attributes the British omission to Treasury cost review rather than a technical inability to fit missiles.

segments.title: How a 15,000-ton cruiser reshapes Washington
segments.summary: A large pre-treaty cruiser raises every category above it and leaves small carriers outside meaningful restriction.
segments.body: A successful 15,000-ton British station cruiser would become the design that treaty negotiators must accommodate. The panel therefore moves the cruiser ceiling to roughly 16,000 tons and suggests a capital-ship limit around 42,000 to 45,000 tons, preserving a clear gap between categories. Carrier limits might rise to about 32,000 tons as well. More consequentially, a rule exempting ships below the cruiser ceiling would now leave capable carriers under 16,000 tons effectively unrestricted. Hermes-like ships could grow faster and more useful, while later light-fleet-carrier dimensions show how much aviation capacity fits beneath that threshold. The treaty intended to contain one expensive surface category would thus encourage numerous small carriers and a very different interwar aviation fleet.
segments.question: How would the proposed 1919 Royal Navy heavy scout cruiser affect the Washington Naval Treaty?
segments.answerShort: It would raise cruiser, carrier, and capital-ship ceilings while making useful carriers below about 16,000 tons effectively treaty-free.

evidence.note: The answer derives higher cruiser, battleship, and carrier limits and explains why Hermes and later light carriers could fall below the new threshold.

segments.title: Agincourt followed by a 28-knot battleship
segments.summary: The Royal Navy would probably build one 18-inch Agincourt, then move to a new 28-knot design rather than repeat it as Waterloo.
segments.body: The panel expects only one ship to be completed as Agincourt in this counterfactual. Although Waterloo would be an attractive sister name, the next capital ship would probably reflect the design direction already emerging before Fisher returned. That direction favors a roughly 28-knot fast battleship rather than a second identical 18-inch ship. Agincourt would therefore stand as a transitional one-off whose successor becomes a distinct class.
segments.question: If the Royal Navy builds an 18-inch HMS Agincourt, does it also build a sister named Waterloo?
segments.answerShort: Probably not; Agincourt remains unique and the next ship becomes a new class of roughly 28-knot fast battleships.

evidence.note: The answer limits the Agincourt design to one ship and places the next construction in a separate 28-knot fast-battleship class.

segments.title: Adolphus Andrews and the Eastern Sea Frontier
segments.summary: Andrews is judged a competent commander working within an American escort shortage created by an earlier-than-expected entry into war.
segments.body: The panel first corrects the premise from the Western to the Eastern Sea Frontier. Andrews had moved through scouting-force and naval-district commands before receiving responsibility for the Atlantic coast organization. His central limitation was not personal ability but the small number of suitable escorts and patrol craft immediately available. American planning had emphasized long-lead major warships and expected a later entry into the conflict, leaving smaller antisubmarine vessels to be ordered closer to the anticipated war date. When the United States entered in 1941, that schedule had not matured, so Andrews is assessed as doing about as well as the available resources permitted.
segments.question: How did Admiral Adolphus Andrews perform as a wartime sea-frontier commander?
segments.answerShort: He handled the Eastern Sea Frontier as well as could be expected, but an early war entry left him short of the small ships needed for coastal defense.

evidence.note: The answer corrects the command, outlines Andrews's assignments, and relates his limited resources to US shipbuilding assumptions and timing.

segments.title: A reading path into Suleyman's Ottoman expansion
segments.summary: Begin with a modern empire-wide survey, move to a specialist edited volume, and use an older narrative history as a readable but dated companion.
segments.body: The panel recommends beginning with Caroline Finkel's Osman's Dream for an accessible history of the Ottoman Empire from its origins into the twentieth century. The 1993 collection Süleymân the Second and His Time, edited by Halil İnalcık and Cemal Kafadar, offers a more focused route into the ruler and his period. Noel Barber's The Lords of the Golden Horn: From Suleiman the Magnificent to Kamal Ataturk provides a broad narrative across later Ottoman history. The answer cautions that the latter reads like a history of the 1970s, making it useful for its narrative while requiring awareness of its age. Together the books form a sensible progression from overview to specialist study and then to an older interpretive account.
segments.question: What are useful starting sources for Suleyman the Magnificent's expansion into Europe?
segments.answerShort: Start with Osman's Dream, add Süleymân the Second and His Time, and read The Lords of the Golden Horn with its age in mind.

evidence.note: The answer recommends a modern overview, a 1993 specialist collection, and an older narrative history with an explicit caveat about its period.

segments.title: Modern special forces at Trafalgar
segments.summary: Modern marksmen could suppress enemy sharpshooters, but misplaced Russian troops would face divided loyalties, language confusion, and cannon fire.
segments.body: The British special-forces team is most useful aboard Victory as a group of long-range marksmen targeting enemy sharpshooters in the rigging. Their modern firearms could protect command personnel without requiring the newcomers to understand sailing or eighteenth-century gunnery. Russian troops placed on Franco-Spanish ships face a basic allegiance problem because Russia was fighting France and Spain in the coalition war. The panel therefore expects confusion followed by the Russians turning their weapons on the crews around them. Modern body armor still offers little protection from cannon shot and heavy splinters, so neither group becomes invulnerable in a line-of-battle engagement.
segments.question: What happens if modern Spetsnaz are placed aboard a Franco-Spanish ship at the Battle of Trafalgar?
segments.answerShort: They likely turn on their nominal hosts amid confusion, while British special forces on Victory would be best employed suppressing enemy sharpshooters.

evidence.note: The panel assigns British marksmen to Victory, considers Russian wartime allegiance and language problems, and tests modern body armor against cannon fire and splinters.

segments.title: Would another navy fire a Royal Navy captain?
segments.summary: Most initiative shown by British captains would be accepted elsewhere; the sharper differences concern political obedience and safeguards for sailors.
segments.body: The panel finds no general Royal Navy practice that would automatically get a captain dismissed by every other service. A commander such as Philip Vian might face greater danger in a navy where disobeying political orders brought immediate punishment, making Nazi Germany the clearest contrast. Japanese and Brazilian command cultures are presented as allowing some conduct that would instead cause trouble in British service, rather than the reverse. The discussion also notes formal Royal Navy safeguards around searching a sailor's quarters, including justification and representation. The comparison is therefore about institutional discipline and rights more than uniquely reckless British seamanship.
segments.question: Did Royal Navy captains do things that would have caused them to be fired in other navies?
segments.answerShort: Usually not; only unusually politicized command systems might punish their initiative, while some foreign practices would be less acceptable in British service.

evidence.note: The answer compares British initiative with German political discipline, Japanese and Brazilian practice, and safeguards during searches of crew quarters.

segments.title: A guided 76 mm barrage against Kongo
segments.summary: Six twin 76 mm mounts firing guided ammunition could strip sensors and command spaces faster than an old battleship could return effective fire.
segments.body: The proposed modern combatant carries six twin OTO Melara 76 mm mounts firing long-range guided ammunition. At the rates used in the scenario, it could deliver about 1,440 rounds in two minutes from roughly forty kilometers. Those shells are not expected to penetrate the main armor belt and sink the battleship directly. Instead, they would destroy exposed sensors, directors, communications, and superstructure, producing a mission kill before the older ship could establish accurate fire. The example distinguishes defeating a warship as a fighting system from physically sending its armored hull to the bottom.
segments.question: How many OTO Melara 76 mm guns would a modern ship need to defeat a Kongo-class battleship?
segments.answerShort: About six twin mounts with guided ammunition could mission-kill it by overwhelming the superstructure, even without piercing the main belt.

evidence.note: The answer estimates mounts, guided-round volume and range, limited return fire, and destruction of the battleship's superstructure and targeting capability.

segments.title: A 1910 Russian Empire dropped into the modern world
segments.summary: Restoring imperial borders and institutions would trigger independence wars, alliance crises, Asian border conflict, economic collapse, and famine.
segments.body: The panel begins with Poland, Finland, Ukraine, and other former imperial territories resisting the sudden loss of modern sovereignty. Depending on how the event is defined, those conflicts could trigger NATO's Article 5 or produce legally ambiguous wars inside transformed borders. Replacing modern institutions and mechanized agriculture with their 1910 equivalents would also remove a major share of contemporary food production and crash global trade. In Asia, imperial claims would collide with modern Chinese borders while the restored Russian state lacked the capacity to defend its enormous territory. The likely result is a multinational war against the empire, followed by further conflict over its breakup and overlapping claims. The answer stresses that the premise changes so many political, administrative, and economic systems at once that no single neat counterfactual outcome is possible.
segments.question: What happens if the territory and institutions of the 1910 Russian Empire suddenly replace the modern states in the same region?
segments.answerShort: It produces wars of independence, NATO and Chinese border crises, global famine, and likely a second struggle over the empire's collapse.

evidence.note: The panel discusses independence wars, Article 5 ambiguity, loss of mechanized agriculture, famine, Chinese border claims, and postwar fragmentation.

segments.title: Historical memory, debt, and political union
segments.summary: The panel contrasts resistance rooted in remembered domination with Scotland's fiscal crisis as two very different pressures on political union.
segments.body: The discussion argues that economic cooperation does not automatically create support for a federal state. Memories of Soviet and Warsaw Pact control make some European societies wary of deeper supranational authority, while Portugal's history with Spain supplies a different long-lived example. The panel then turns to Scotland after the Darien scheme and presents severe debt as a pressure behind the 1707 union with England. In that account, fiscal weakness affected Scotland's ability to maintain military and naval capacity and made outside assumption of debt increasingly important. The comparison is not that every crisis creates a union, but that political integration becomes possible only when security, economic pressure, and historical identity align in a particular way. It also shows why a common external threat may strengthen cooperation without erasing fears of centralized rule.

evidence.note: The panel connects resistance to European federation with historical memory, then uses the Darien crisis, debt, defense capacity, and the Act of Union as a contrasting case.
